title: Two chai strings walk into a comparison

options: a == b is true and a.equals(b) is true
a == b is false but a.equals(b) is true
a == b is true but a.equals(b) is false
Both are false — they're different objects
mcq.explanation_md: `new String(...)` forces Java to create a **separate object** each time, so `a` and `b` are two different objects holding the same text. `==` compares references — same object or not? — so it's `false`. `.equals()` compares the actual characters, so it's `true`. The tempting answer is "both true": that's what you'd see with plain literals (`String a = "chai";`), because identical literals share one pooled object — which is exactly why this trap works. The rule that never fails: compare String *content* with `.equals()`, always.
